title: Navigating Work Relationships During Kundalini Crisis

body:
Navigating Work Relationships During Kundalini Crisis

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You're probably staring at the ceiling, feeling like your insides are vibrating, or maybe you're just numb, completely detached, and the thought of facing another day at work makes your stomach churn. I know that feeling. I've been there. The fear, the isolation, the sheer terror of thinking you're losing your mind, and then having to put on a brave face, sit in meetings, and pretend everything's fine. It's a special kind of hell, isn't it? Especially when your very being feels like it's being ripped apart and reassembled, sometimes violently. And you've got bills to pay, right? That's the kicker. You can't just check out. You have to keep going, even when every cell in your body is screaming for you to stop.

Look, I'm not going to sugarcoat this for you. Kundalini crisis - or spiritual emergency, whatever label you wanna slap on it - it's messy. It's disorienting. It's not a walk in the park. And trying to hold down a job, maintain relationships with colleagues, and act like a functioning adult while this is happening? It's one of the hardest things you'll ever do. But you're not alone. I promise you that. You're not the first, and you won't be the last. I've seen it. I've lived it. So let's talk about how you can survive this, how you can keep your job, and maybe, just maybe, keep a shred of your sanity while the universe rearranges your internal furniture.

The Internal Chaos vs. External Demands

You're feeling like a raw nerve. Every sound is too loud, every light too bright. Your thoughts are racing, or maybe they're completely absent, leaving you with this terrifying void. You might be having visions, hearing things, feeling energy surges that make you want to jump out of your skin. And then your boss asks you for that report, or a colleague wants to chat about the weekend. How do you even begin to bridge that gap? How do you pretend to care about TPS reports when you're convinced you're either ascending to another dimension or descending into madness?

Here's the thing: you can't completely bridge it. Not perfectly. You're operating on a different plane, even if no one else sees it. Your priorities have shifted dramatically. What used to matter might feel utterly trivial now. This disconnect is what creates so much of the suffering. You're constantly code-switching, trying to fit your vast, chaotic internal experience into the tiny, neat box of professional expectations. It's exhausting. It's soul-crushing. But it's also a skill you're going to have to develop. You're learning to be a master of camouflage, whether you like it or not.

Managing Energy and Sensory Overload

One of the biggest challenges is the sheer energetic and sensory assault. Your nervous system is basically on fire. Normal office chatter can feel like a cacophony. Fluorescent lights can feel like they're drilling into your brain. The smell of someone's lunch can be overwhelming. You're hypersensitive. This isn't just "being stressed"; it's a fundamental shift in how you process reality. So, what do you do?

  • Create your sanctuary: Even if it's just your desk. Keep it clean. Minimize clutter. If you can, use noise-canceling headphones. I know, I know, it might look antisocial, but honestly, your sanity is more important than appearances right now. Play some calming instrumental music if you can get away with it.
  • Take frequent, short breaks: Get up. Walk away from your desk. Go to the bathroom and splash water on your face. Step outside for five minutes if possible, even if it's just to breathe fresh air. These aren't luxuries; they're necessities to reset your overloaded system.
  • Manage your schedule: If you have any control over your schedule, try to front-load demanding tasks for when you feel your best, which might be a small window. If you're having energy surges or crashes, try to schedule less mentally taxing work during those times.
  • Breathing exercises: Sounds simple, right? But it's powerful. Just five deep breaths, slow and steady, can sometimes pull you back from the brink of a panic attack. Do it discreetly. No one needs to know you're fighting an internal war.
  • Grounding techniques: Keep something tangible with you - a smooth stone, a piece of wood, anything you can hold and focus on the texture. It helps bring you back to your body, to the present moment, when your mind is flying off into the ether.

These aren't cures, but they're tools. They're ways to cope, to survive the workday. You're in survival mode, and that's okay. Give yourself permission to do what you need to do to get through it.

Communicating Without Revealing Too Much

This is where it gets tricky. You can't exactly tell your boss, "Hey, I'm having a kundalini awakening and I might spontaneously combust during the quarterly review." Right? They'd probably think you've lost it, and honestly, you might even think you've lost it. So, you need to learn to communicate your needs without spilling all your spiritual guts. It's about setting boundaries and managing expectations subtly.

  • "I'm not feeling 100% today": This is your go-to. It's vague, it's acceptable, and it explains a lot. You don't need to elaborate. Most people will just nod and move on.
  • "I need some quiet time to focus": Use this to excuse yourself from unnecessary conversations or to justify wearing those headphones.
  • "I'm a bit overwhelmed right now": If someone piles on work, you can use this to ask for an extension or to prioritize tasks. Again, no need to explain why you're overwhelmed.
  • Be brief and direct: When you do have to interact, keep it short. Don't get drawn into long conversations if you can avoid them. Your energy is a precious, finite resource right now. Conserve it.

It's about protecting your energy and your perception. You don't owe anyone an explanation for your internal experience. You just owe them competence at your job, and you're doing your best to maintain that amidst unusual circumstances. Know what I mean? It's a tightrope walk.

Colleagues: Allies, Acquaintances, or Avoidance?

This is probably one of the most isolating parts. You're surrounded by people, but you feel utterly alone. How do you deal with colleagues when your reality feels so different from theirs?

You're not going to find many allies who truly understand what you're going through, honestly. That's just the truth. Most people haven't a clue about kundalini, and if you try to explain it, you might just scare them off. So, your strategy here is primarily about self-preservation and maintaining a professional front.

  • Keep it professional: This is your mantra. Your work relationships aren't the place for deep spiritual sharing right now. Save that for your therapist, your spiritual guide, or your trusted, understanding friends - if you have any who "get it."
  • Avoid oversharing: Don't talk about your visions, your energy surges, your existential dread. It's not their burden, and they're not equipped to handle it. You might think you're finding common ground, but you're more likely to just make them uncomfortable or worried about your stability.
  • Be polite, but distant: You don't have to be a hermit, but you don't need to be the life of the office party either. A polite nod, a brief "How are you?" is usually enough. You're conserving your social energy.
  • Identify safe people (carefully): If there's someone at work who seems genuinely open-minded, perhaps they've mentioned something spiritual before, you might, might, consider a very, very light and vague hint. "I'm going through a really intense personal transformation right now," or "I'm having some unusual experiences." But be prepared for blank stares or awkward silence. Don't push it. The vast majority won't get it.

I remember trying to explain some of what I was feeling to a colleague once. Their eyes just glazed over. It was like I was speaking a different language. That's when I learned to shut up about it at work. It's not their fault; they just don't have the frame of reference. It's not a personal rejection; it's just a reality of this path. You're walking a path that most people aren't even aware exists. That's why books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) can be such a lifeline - they validate what you're going through, even if no one around you does.

Maintaining Functionality: The Non-Negotiables

You're not aiming for peak performance right now. You're aiming for functional. You're aiming for "not getting fired." That's your goal. And to do that, there are a few non-negotiables you need to stick to, even when your inner world is screaming.

  • Do your job: This sounds obvious, but when you're dissociating or having intense internal experiences, it's easy to let things slip. Focus on the bare minimum if you have to. Prioritize. What absolutely HAS to get done? Do that.
  • Be on time: Punctuality shows you're still engaged and reliable, even if you feel like you're floating three feet above your body.
  • Meet deadlines (or communicate early): If you know you can't meet a deadline, communicate it well in advance. "I'm having trouble with X, can I get an extension?" is much better than missing it entirely.
  • Stay organized (as much as possible): Even if your mind feels like scrambled eggs, try to keep your physical workspace organized. It helps create a sense of external order when your internal world is chaotic.
  • Take care of basic hygiene: This sounds basic, but when you're in crisis, it's easy to let things slide. Shower. Brush your teeth. Wear clean clothes. It helps you feel a tiny bit more grounded, and it prevents colleagues from noticing any outward signs of distress.

You're showing up. That's a victory in itself. You're doing the work. You're keeping your head above water. That's all anyone can ask of you right now. And honestly, it's a huge accomplishment when you're fighting an invisible battle within yourself. Reading accounts from others, like in The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link), can show you that you're not alone in these struggles to maintain normalcy.

Seeking Support (Outside of Work)

You absolutely cannot do this alone. I tried. It almost broke me. You need people who understand, or at least who are willing to listen without judgment. This support network is going to be your lifeline.

  • Find a therapist or spiritual counselor: Someone who understands spiritual emergency or transpersonal psychology. This isn't just regular therapy; it's specialized. They won't think you're crazy. They'll help you integrate these experiences. This is probably the single most important step you can take.
  • Connect with others who've been through it: Online forums, support groups (if you can find one in your area). Just knowing there are others like you, others who've survived, is incredibly validating.
  • Trusted friends or family: Choose wisely. Not everyone can handle this. Pick the one or two people in your life who are truly open-minded, compassionate, and won't try to "fix" you or tell you you're delusional.
  • Books, books, books: Reading about others' experiences can be incredibly comforting. It helps you normalize what feels utterly abnormal. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) are excellent starting points. They offer guidance and reassurance from someone who's not only studied it but lived it.

You need a safe space to vent, to process, to feel heard without judgment. Your workplace is not that space. Your colleagues are not that space. That's a harsh reality, but it's one you need to accept for your own protection. Does that land? You're protecting your job, yes, but you're also protecting your fragile internal state from misunderstanding and potential harm. This journey is intensely personal, and you're going to need a very specific kind of support to get through it.

The Long Game: Acceptance and Integration

This isn't a sprint; it's a marathon. Or maybe it's more like being caught in a spiritual hurricane. It's going to take time. There will be good days and absolutely terrible days. You'll feel like you're making progress, and then you'll crash hard. That's normal. That's part of the process. You're not failing; you're just experiencing the ebb and flow of this intense transformation.

Your goal, eventually, is integration. It's not about going back to "normal," because that normal is gone. It's about finding a new normal, where your spiritual experiences and your everyday life can coexist. You'll learn to live with the energy, to understand its rhythms, and to even appreciate the deep shifts it's bringing about. But that's later. For now, you're just surviving. And that's okay. Give yourself grace. You're doing something incredibly difficult, something most people will never experience. You're a warrior, even if you feel like a scared kid in the dark.

So yeah, you're going to have to put on that mask for work. You're going to have to pretend you're okay when you're anything but. You're going to have to be strategic with your energy and your words. But you're doing it to protect yourself, to keep a roof over your head, and to create the space you need to heal and integrate. You're stronger than you think. You really are. Just keep putting one foot in front of the other, and remember, you're not alone in this dark night of the soul.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
